2-6.q: All of the following statements concerning standard costs are correct except that:
o: Time and motion studies are often used to determine standard costs
Standard costs are usually set for one year
Standard costs are usually stated in total, while budgeted costs are usually stated on a per-unit basis
Standard costs can be used in costing inventory accounts
2-6.e: This is the correct answer because it is not a correct statement. The statement is actually flipped around. Standards are stated on a per unit basis, while budgeted figures are stated in total.
wrongWhy: These studies are often used to determine how long it usually takes to perform a given task. That "usual time" becomes the standard.
Standard costs are reviewed periodically to account for any market changes. Annually during budget time is frequently the review period for standard costs.

When standard costs are used, items are added to inventory at their standard costs. Variances between actual incurred costs and the standard costs may be resolved by adjusting both inventories and cost of goods sold; or they may be resolved by adjusting only cost of goods sold. But the initial debit to inventory is for the standard cost amount. Therefore, this is a true statement. 4.

2-6.q: Jura Corporation is developing standards for the next year. Currently XZ-26, one of the material components, is being purchased for $36.45 per unit. It is expected that the component’s cost will increase by approximately 10% next year and the price could range from $38.75 to $44.18 per unit depending on the quantity purchased. The appropriate standard for XZ-26 for next year should be set at the
o: lowest purchase price in the anticipated range to keep pressure on purchasing to always buy in the lowest price range
price agreed upon by the purchasing manager and the appropriate level of company management
current actual cost plus the forecasted 10% price increase
highest price in the anticipated range to insure that there are only favorable purchase price variances
2-6.e: Discussions between relevant managers to determine the exact quantities required and the timing of those purchases will help determine what price level would be most appropriate for the standard. This is the participative method of standard setting, and it is more likely to be supported by the employees than an authoritative method of standard setting.
wrongWhy: Standards are set using an authoritative process, a participative process, or a consultative process. Standards based on anticipated costs may be a starting point for discussion using any process, but there is no absolute standard that should be used in this situation. Setting a standard in order to keep pressure on an employee or group of employees is not a participative process. Furthermore, pressure to obtain the lowest price may encourage the purchase of excess inventory, which creates increased inventory carrying costs.

Standards are set using an authoritative process, a participative process, or a consultative process. Standards based on historical costs plus an expected price increase may be a starting point for discussion using any process, but there is no absolute standard that should be used in this situation.

Standards should never be set to result in favorable variances. To do that renders the standard setting process useless from the beginning. Standards need to be based on expected reality, considering all appropriate factors. 5.

2-6.q: When compared with ideal standards, practical standards:
o: Serve as a better motivating target for manufacturing personnel
Incorporate very generous allowances for spoilage and worker inefficiencies
Result in a less desirable basis for the development of budgets
2-6.e: Ideal, or theoretical, standards are the level of activity that will occur if the company produces at its absolute most efficient level at all times, with no allowances made for idle time or downtime. Ideal standards cannot be achieved in the long run. Practical standards are the ideal capacity reduced by allowances for idle time and downtime. Because practical standards are achievable, they are a better target to motivate manufacturing employees. Knowing the volume that both can and should be produced gives employees a realistic guideline that can be applied to everyday working conditions. Practical standards are reasonable, not impossible.
wrongWhy: Practical standards do allow for downtime or idle time, but those allowances do not include incompetence or irresponsible use of resources.
A practical standard includes allowances for downtime and idle time. Therefore it would actually increase the per-unit standard costs somewhat.

Practical standards are a more desirable basis as rarely does any company work under ideal situations. To budget at those levels would be to set the company up for multiple budget variances and unmotivated employees. 6.

2-6.q: A company’s budgeting process for direct labor begins by determining an average wage rate for all its production employees when manufacturing one unit of production. This rate becomes part of the company’s budgeted standard cost for direct labor. A management consultant who advised the company on its budgeting process said that when determining the budgeted standard cost for direct labor, the wage rate
o: can be defined only after determining the budgeted standard cost for direct materials
should be determined only after the quantity standard for direct labor is defined
can be calculated at any point during the budgeting process
is calculated at the end of the budgeting process once all relevant costs are known
2-6.e: The standard wage rate varies according to the type of employees needed and the skill level required. The quantity standard for direct labor includes the skill level needed for each class of direct labor. For example, operation of a computerized piece of equipment requires an operator who understands the operation of the computer. When the skill level required for each class of labor that will be needed has been determined, the standard wage rate can be determined for each class of labor as a function of the competitive labor market and any labor contracts that may exist.
wrongWhy: The standard cost for direct materials is not relevant for setting the direct labor wage rate. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
The direct labor wage rate needs to be determined before the direct labor usage budget is prepared as part of the operating budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
The direct labor wage rate needs to be determined before the direct labor usage budget is prepared as part of the operating budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 7.

2-6.q: Which one of the following is least likely to be involved in establishing standard costs for evaluation purposes?
2-6.e: Top management is primarily involved in formulating strategy plans and budgets. Establishing standard costs for evaluation purposes is a task of management of a lower level. These standards are used to estimate what costs should be under normal conditions of operations. Industrial engineers, budgetary accountants, quality control personnel and employees who will be evaluated using these criteria are involved in the process.
wrongWhy: Quality control personnel are involved in establishing standard costs for evaluation purposes.
Budgetary accountants are involved in establishing standard costs for evaluation purposes.
Industrial engineers are involved in establishing standard costs for evaluation purposes. 8.

2-6.q: Granger Company is reviewing its standard machine hours per unit to use in its budget for the upcoming year. The machine manufacturer's specifications indicated a unit could be made in 0.75 hours, and a benchmarking study showed a competitor produced at a speed of 0.78 machine hours per unit. Granger's actual results from last year averaged 0.83 machine hours per unit even though a standard of 0.80 machine hours per unit had been established using engineering studies. The standard Granger should use in its upcoming budget is
2-6.e: Last year, the standard of 0.80 machine hours per unit was not met. However, the standard had been developed using engineering studies, and it is even higher than the machine manufacturer's specifications, which indicate that a unit can be made in 0.75 hours. There is no reason to increase the standard simply because it was not met in the previous year. Standards should be challenging but attainable, and a standard of 0.80 machine hours per unit is attainable. At the same time, however, if the standard was not met last year, there is little justification for decreasing the standard further this year. Therefore, the standard should remain at 0.80 hours per unit, and the employees should strive to meet that standard this year.
wrongWhy: Last year, the standard of 0.80 machine hours per unit was not met. Since the standard was not met last year, there is little justification for decreasing the standard further this year.
Last year, the standard of 0.80 machine hours per unit was not met. Since the standard was not met last year, there is little justification for decreasing the standard further this year.

Last year, the standard of 0.80 machine hours per unit was not met. However, the standard had been developed using engineering studies, and it is even higher than the machine manufacturer's specifications, which indicate that a unit can be made in 0.75 hours. There is no reason to increase the standard simply because it was not met in the previous year. 1.

2-6.q: Which type of standard is Most commonly used for budgeting and cost control?
2-6.e: Practical (Attainable) standards are most commonly used — challenging but achievable with Normal effort and efficiency. They allow for expected inefficiencies (breakdowns, rest time) unlike ideal standards.

2-6.q: Direct Materials Price Variance = (Actual Price - Standard Price) × Actual Quantity. If actual price is Less than standard, the variance is:
2-6.e: If Actual Price < Standard Price → we paid Less than expected → Favorable variance. The company saved money on material purchasing compared to the standard (budgeted) price.

2-6.q: The Direct Labor Efficiency Variance measures:
o: The difference between actual and standard wage rates
Whether actual labor hours were more or less than standard for actual output
The total DL cost vs budget
2-6.e: DL Efficiency Variance = (Actual Hours - Standard Hours Allowed) × Standard Rate. It measures Labor Time Efficiency — whether workers took more or fewer hours than the standard for the actual output produced.

2-6.q: Standard costs are Best described as:
o: Historical average costs from prior years
Predetermined planned costs based on normal operating conditions
2-6.e: Standard costs = Predetermined planned costs under Normal operating conditions. They are set in advance (before production begins) and serve as benchmarks for budgeting and performance evaluation.

2-6.q: Ideal (theoretical) standards assume:
o: Perfect efficiency — no waste, breakdowns, or idle time
2-6.e: Ideal standards = Perfect conditions — no waste, no breakdowns, no idle time. They represent the maximum possible efficiency. Rarely achieved in practice and generally not used for practical budgeting.

2-6.q: The standard quantity for DM should include:
o: Only the material that ends up in the final product
The required amount plus normal waste and spoilage
2-6.e: Standard DM quantity = material needed for the product Plus Normal Waste And Spoilage. Using only the theoretical minimum ignores expected losses and would create unrealistic standards.

2-6.q: An Unfavorable DM Quantity Variance indicates that:
o: Material was purchased above standard price
More material was Used than standard allowed for actual output
Less material was used than standard
2-6.e: Unfavorable DM Quantity Variance = Actual Quantity Used > Standard Quantity Allowed for actual output. Workers used More material than standard — may indicate waste, spoilage, poor quality materials, or inexperienced workers.

2-6.q: If standard labor rate = $15/hr, actual rate = $17/hr, and actual hours = 500, DL Rate Variance =
2-6.e: DL Rate Variance = (Actual Rate - Standard Rate) × Actual Hours = ($17 - $15) × 500 = $2 × 500 = $1,000 Unfavorable (actual rate > standard = paid more than expected).

2-6.q: Standard DL rate per hour includes:
2-6.e: Standard DL rate = Base Wage + payroll taxes + employee benefits. It represents the full cost of one hour of direct labor, not just the base wage. This gives a complete standard cost per hour.

2-6.q: Variance analysis is Primarily used for:
o: Performance control — identifying why actual costs differ from standard
2-6.e: Variance analysis is a Management Control tool. It identifies Where and Why actual costs differ from standards, enabling management to investigate causes, take corrective action, and improve future performance.

2-6.q: Direct Labor Rate Variance = (Actual Rate - Standard Rate) × Actual Hours. If actual rate Exceeds standard rate:
o: Unfavorable — company paid More than the standard wage
2-6.e: DL Rate Variance Unfavorable when Actual Rate > Standard Rate — company paid More per hour than budgeted. Possible causes: overtime premium, use of higher-skill workers than planned, wage increases not reflected in standard.

2-6.q: A Favorable DM Quantity Variance means:
o: Materials were purchased above standard price
Materials were purchased below standard price
Less material was used than the standard allowed for actual output
More material was used than standard
2-6.e: Favorable DM Quantity Variance = Actual Qty Used < Standard Qty Allowed. The company used Less material than the standard allowed for the actual production achieved. Could indicate better material quality or improved production efficiency.

2-7.q: An electronics company has developed a regression model to forecast quarterly sales. The model explains the relationship between the company’s sales and the amount it spends on marketing activities. The regression equation for the model is expressed below. s = $3(m) + $150,000 s = sales per quarter m = dollars spent on marketing activities per quarter If the company has forecasted sales of $189,000 for the next quarter, what amount is it planning to spend on marketing activities in the next quarter?
2-7.e: This is an example of causal forecasting. The regression equation as given in the problem is used to solve for m, the amount planned to be spent on marketing activities. 3m + 150,000 = 189,000 Step 1: Subtract 150,000 from both sides of the equation: 3m = 39,000 Step 2: Divide both sides of the equation by 3 to isolate the m: m = 13,000 The company is planning to spend $13,000 on marketing activities in the next quarter.
wrongWhy: This is the sum of $189,000 in forecasted sales and $150,000 from the regression equation divided by 3, also from the regression equation. The regression equation as given in the problem should be used to solve for m, the amount planned to be spent on marketing activities. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is the forecasted sales of $189,000 divided by 3 from the regression equation. The regression equation as given in the problem should be used to solve for m, the amount planned to be spent on marketing activities. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is the forecasted sales of $189,000 minus the $150,000 in the regression equation. The regression equation as given in the problem should be used to solve for m, the amount planned to be spent on marketing activities. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 2.

2-7.q: For cost estimation simple regression differs from multiple regression in that simple regression uses only
o: one dependent variable, while multiple regression uses all available data to estimate the cost function
dependent variables, while multiple regression can use both dependent and independent variables
one dependent variable, while multiple regression uses more than one dependent variable
one independent variable, while multiple regression uses more than one independent variable. this question
2-7.e: Simple regression analysis uses one independent variable to estimate or forecast the dependent variable, whereas multiple regression analysis uses more than one independent variable to estimate or forecast the dependent variable.
wrongWhy: Both simple regression analysis and multiple regression analysis are used to estimate or forecast one dependent variable.
Both simple regression analysis and multiple regression analysis use both dependent and independent variables.
Both simple regression analysis and multiple regression analysis are used to estimate or forecast one dependent variable. 3.

2-7.q: In the standard regression equation ŷ = a + bx, the letter b is best described as a(n)
2-7.e: In the standard regression equation as represented here, the b in the equation represents the variable coefficient, or the amount of increase in ŷ for each unit of increase in x, which is the slope of the line.
wrongWhy: The constant coefficient is represented by a in the regression equation given. It represents the y intercept, because this is the value of y when x = 0.
The independent variable is represented by x in the regression equation given.
The dependent variable is represented by y in the regression equation given. 4.

2-7.q: A distinction between forecasting and planning
o: Is not valid because they are synonyms
Is that forecasting is a management activity whereas planning is a technical activity
Is that forecasts are used in planning
Arises because forecasting covers the short-term and planning does not. this question
2-7.e: Planning is the process of determining how to achieve the company's goals and objectives, or the questions of what, how, when, where and who for the company's operations. This plan is then communicated to all of the company to serve as a guide for future actions. A forecast is an attempt to determine the future activity levels or environment that the company will be operating in. Forecasts are used in the planning process as a basis for some of the decisions that need to be made.
wrongWhy: Though these terms may often be used or thought of as synonyms, they are not. Planning is the process of determining how to achieve the company's goals and objectives, or the questions of what, how, when, where and who for the company's operations. This plan is then communicated to all of the company to serve as a guide for future actions. A forecast is an attempt to determine the future activity levels or environment that the company will be operating in.
Forecasting is more of technical activity than planning because of the mathematical models that may be used in the forecasting process. In any case, planning is certainly a management activity.
Both planning and forecasting can be used in either a short-term or long-term time frame. 5.

2-7.q: The letter x in the standard regression equation is best described as a(n)
2-7.e: In the standard regression equation, x represents the independent variable.
wrongWhy: The coefficient of determination is not a part of the standard regression equation at all. The coefficient of determination is the percentage of the total amount of change in the dependent variable that can be explained by changes in the independent variable.
In the standard regression equation, the dependent variable is represented by y.

The constant coefficient is represented by a letter that stands by itself, that is, a letter without an x term next to it. The constant coefficient is the y intercept, because that is the value of y when x = 0. 1.

2-7.q: The Delphi method of forecasting uses:
o: Anonymous expert opinions through rounds of questionnaires
Sales force estimates from the field
2-7.e: Delphi method: Anonymous expert panel answers questionnaires in Rounds — results are summarized and fed back, experts revise their answers. Anonymous process prevents Groupthink and dominant personalities from biasing results.

2-7.q: R² = 0.85 in a regression analysis means:
o: 85% of the variation in cost is explained by the independent variable
2-7.e: R² = Coefficient Of Determination. R² = 0.85 means 85% of the Variation in the dependent variable (Y) is Explained by the independent variable (X). Higher R² = better fit.

2-7.q: Exponential smoothing gives:
o: Equal weight to all historical periods
Weight only to the highest and lowest periods
2-7.e: Exponential smoothing gives Decreasing Weights to older observations — most recent data gets the highest weight. This makes the forecast Responsive to recent changes while still considering historical patterns.

2-7.q: Time series decomposition identifies which components?
o: Trend, seasonal, cyclical, and random variations
Fixed, variable, mixed, and step costs
2-7.e: Time series components: Trend (long-term direction), Seasonal (within-year patterns), Cyclical (multi-year economic cycles), Random/Irregular (unpredictable noise). Decomposing these helps build more accurate forecasts.

2-7.q: The regression equation Y = $5,000 + $3X predicts total cost at 2,000 machine hours as:
The Most accurate quantitative forecasting method is:
2-7.e: Regression Analysis is the most accurate quantitative method — it uses All historical data points objectively (least squares technique) to find the best-fit relationship. Moving averages use fewer data points and are less accurate.

2-7.q: A Correlation Coefficient (R) of -0.95 indicates:
2-7.e: R = -0.95: close to -1 = Strong Negative relationship. As X increases, Y decreases strongly. The negative sign indicates direction; the magnitude (0.95 close to 1) indicates Strength.

2-7.q: Sales force composite forecasting is:
o: Statistical analysis of historical sales data
Bottom-up estimation by sales representatives for their territories
2-7.e: Sales force composite: Bottom-Up approach — individual sales representatives estimate sales for their territories, then aggregated to a total. Leverages field knowledge but may contain bias (pessimism or optimism).

2-7.q: A p-value of 0.03 in regression analysis indicates:
o: The relationship is not statistically significant
2-7.e: p-value < 0.05 = Statistically Significant — the relationship is unlikely to be due to chance (only 3% probability). p-value ≥ 0.05 = relationship not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level.

2-7.q: Which forecasting method is Best for products with strong seasonal patterns?
o: Time series analysis with seasonal decomposition
2-7.e: Time series analysis with Seasonal Decomposition is best for seasonal patterns — it explicitly identifies and models the seasonal component, allowing the forecast to capture regular within-year variations.

2-7.q: Simple (linear) regression predicts costs using:
2-7.e: Simple regression uses One independent variable (cost driver) to predict the dependent variable (cost). Example: predicting total cost from machine hours. Multiple regression uses two or more independent variables for greater accuracy.

2-7.q: A moving average forecast Smooths data by:
o: Giving more weight to recent periods
Giving equal weight to all periods in the moving window
2-7.e: Moving average smooths data by averaging the Most Recent N periods with Equal Weight. As each new period is added, the oldest period is dropped. This removes short-term fluctuations but does not adjust for trends or seasonality.

2-7.q: A naive forecast uses:
o: The Most Recent actual period as the forecast for the next period
2-7.e: Naive forecast = the Prior Period'S Actual as the next period's forecast. Simplest possible method. Useful only in very stable, non-seasonal environments. Low cost but low accuracy.

2-7.q: Qualitative forecasting methods are Most useful when:
o: Large amounts of historical data are available
Launching a New product with No historical data
2-7.e: Qualitative methods (Delphi, jury of executive opinion, consumer surveys) are best when Historical Data IS Unavailable — particularly for New products, new markets, or rapidly changing conditions where past data cannot reliably predict the future.

2-7.q: Jury of executive opinion forecasting involves:
o: Senior Executives pooling their judgment to reach a consensus forecast
2-7.e: Jury Of Executive Opinion = senior executives pool their expertise and experience to reach a consensus forecast. Quick and draws on diverse experience. Risk: dominant personalities may skew results — unlike Delphi's anonymous process.

2-8.q: Martin Fabricating uses a cumulative average-time learning curve model to monitor labor costs. Data regarding two recently completed batches of a part that is used in tractor-trailer rigs is as follows. Batch Number Cumulative Average Number of Units Hours Per Unit 1 50 20 2 50 16 If the same rate of learning continues for the next several batches produced, which of the following best describes (1) the type (i.e., degree) of learning curve that the firm is experiencing and (2) the average hours per unit for units included in the 201-400 range of units produced (i.e., the last 200 units)?
o: Learning curve - 80%; average hours per unit for units 201-400 - 7.68 hours
Learning curve - 20%; average hours per unit for units 201-400 - 10.24 hours
Learning curve - 20%; average hours per unit for units 201-400 - 3.84 hours
Learning curve - 80%; average hours per unit for units 201-400 - 10.24 hours
2-8.e: The cumulative average learning curve is 80%, calculated as the cumulative time required for the first 100 units (the first doubling) divided by two times the cumulative time required for the first 50 units. (16 × 100) / [2 × (20 × 50)] = 1,600 / 2,000 = 0.80 or 80%. The way to calculate the average time per unit required to produce the last 200 units (units 201-400) is to (1) calculate the total time required for 400 units [8 batches]; (2) calculate the total time required for 200 units [4 batches]; (3) subtract the total time for 200 units from the total time for 400 units to find the total time for the last 200 units; and (4) divide the result of #3 by 200 units to find the average time per unit for the last 200 units. The amount of time required to produce the first batch of 50 units is 50 × 20, or 1,000 hours. Using the formula for total time required, which is: Time Required for the First Unit(s) × (2 × LC)n The total time required for 400 units = 1,000 × (2 × 0.80)3 = 4,096. The total time required for 200 units = 1,000 × (2 × 0.80)2 = 2,560. The total time required for units 201-400 (the last 200 units) = 4,096 − 2,560 = 1,536. The average time per unit required to produce units 201-400 = 1,536 / 200 = 7.68 hours.
wrongWhy: 20% is not a possible learning curve. Learning curves must always be greater than 50% (and less than 100%). If the learning curve percentage were less than 50%, it would imply that the total of the time required to produce the additional units when production doubles and the time required to produce the initial unit(s) is less than the time required for production of the initial unit(s), and that is not possible. 10.24 hours is the average time per unit required to produce 400 units. The question asks for the average time per unit required to product the last 200 units, units 201-400. The way to calculate the answer to this question is to (1) calculate the total time required for 400 units [8 batches]; (2) calculate the total time required for 200 units [4 batches]; (3) subtract the total time for 200 units from the total time for 400 units to find the total time for the last 200 units; and (4) divide the result of #3 by 200 units to find the average time per unit for the last 200 units.

20% is not a possible learning curve. Learning curves must always be greater than 50% (and less than 100%). If the learning curve percentage were less than 50%, it would imply that the total of the time required to produce the additional units when production doubles and the time required to produce the initial unit(s) is less than the time required for production of the initial unit(s), and that is not possible. 3.84 hours is the total number of hours required to produce units 201-400 divided by 400. There are only 200 units in the group of units numbered 201-400.

10.24 hours is the average time per unit required to produce 400 units. The question asks for the average time per unit required to product the last 200 units, units 201-400. The way to calculate the answer to this question is to (1) calculate the total time required for 400 units [8 batches]; (2) calculate the total time required for 200 units [4 batches]; (3) subtract the total time for 200 units from the total time for 400 units to find the total time for the last 200 units; and (4) divide the result of #3 by 200 units to find the average time per unit for the last 200 units. 2.

2-8.q: Which one of the following techniques would most likely be used to analyze reductions in the time required to perform a task as experience with that task increases?
2-8.e: Learning curve analysis is be used to analyze reductions in the time required to perform a task as experience with that task increases.
wrongWhy: Sensitivity analysis is used to determine how an expected result can be expected to change if factors that were involved in predicting the result change.

"Normal probability analysis" probably refers to the concept of "normal distribution," since there is no such thing as "normal probability analysis." A normal distribution in statistics is a frequency distribution that is in the shape of a classic bell curve.
Regression analysis is a statistical technique used to find relationships between variables in order to predict future values. 3.

2-8.q: A manufacturing firm plans to bid on a special order of 80 units that will be manufactured in lots of 10 units each. The production manager estimates that the direct labor hours per unit will decline by a constant percentage each time the cumulative quantity of units produced doubles. The quantitative technique used to capture this phenomenon and estimate the direct labor hours required for the special order is
2-8.e: Learning curve analysis would be used to estimate the direct labor hours required for the special order because it captures the decline in direct labor hours required that would occur each time the cumulative quantity of units produced doubles.
wrongWhy: Cost-volume-profit analysis is used for short-run decision-making by firms to determine what products they will supply and the amount they will supply at a given price and cost.

Linear programming can be used to allocate a scarce resource in a system in which there are constraints such as limited quantities of some input, with the goal of either maximizing or minimizing some quantity without violating the constraints.

Markov process models are used to study systems and changes that take place in the systems during repeated trials. The information is used to make decisions because it can help determine the probabilities for the occurrence of certain events. For instance, one type of Markov analysis is used to determine the probability of something happening again tomorrow if the same thing happened today. It might be used to determine the probability that a machine that is functioning in the current period will continue to function in the next period or whether it will break down. 4.

2-8.q: Learning curve analysis is most common in companies that
o: seek to forecast linear cost behavior in their production operations
manufacture in highly automated plants with high start-up costs
operate in labor-intensive industries with repetitive tasks and long production runs
are trying to improve labor productivity through increased automation
2-8.e: “Learning curve” refers to the concept that workers' efficiency increases as the amount of experience they have with a given task increases; and the time required for performing the task decreases as the number of times the task has been performed increases. Therefore, learning curve analysis would be most relevant in companies with labor-intensive production processes utilizing repetitive tasks and long production runs where employees have significant opportunities to perform the same tasks over and over.
wrongWhy: Linear cost behavior involves total costs that increase in a linear fashion (that is, as a straight line on a graph) depending on the quantity produced, meaning the cost per unit does not change. A learning curve results in decreasing direct labor costs per unit because of increased efficiency as employees' experience with a task grows. Thus, when a learning curve is applicable, the total labor cost line on a graph would be in the shape of a curve, not a straight line. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

“Learning curve” refers to the concept that workers' efficiency increases as the amount of experience they have with a given task increases; and the time required for performing the task decreases as the number of times the task has been performed increases. Learning curve analysis would not be relevant in a highly automated manufacturing environment because there would not be many workers, and the operating speed of a machine does not increase as a result of increased production. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

“Learning curve” refers to the concept that workers' efficiency increases as the amount of experience they have with a given task increases; and the time required for performing the task decreases as the number of times the task has been performed increases. Productivity improvements resulting from increased automation would not come about as a result of a learning curve, because the operating speed of a machine does not increase as a result of increased production. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 5.

2-8.q: A software company recently established a customer service department. After the first week, the average time required to handle one customer call was 15 minutes. The manager of the customer service department estimates an 80% learning curve. Under the cumulative average-time learning model, the cumulative average time required to handle one customer call after the fourth week will be
2-8.e: The formula for calculating the estimated cumulative average time for all units is: Estimated cumulative average time per unit = Time required for first unit × LCn, where: LC = Learning curve percentage (in decimal format) n = Number of doublings of all units After the fourth week, the time will have doubled twice (once from 1 week to 2 weeks and once from 2 weeks to four weeks). Therefore: Estimated cumulative average time per unit = 15 × 0.802 Estimated cumulative average time per unit = 9.6 minutes
wrongWhy: This answer results from using three doublings to calculate the estimated average time per unit after four weeks. However, after the fourth week, the time will have doubled twice, not three times. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from using one doubling to calculate the estimated average time per unit after four weeks. However, after the fourth week, the time will have doubled twice, not once. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from assuming no learning takes place at all, and thus the average time required to handle one customer call after four weeks will be the same as the average time required after one week. However, the manager of the customer service department estimates an 80% learning curve, so the average time after four weeks will be lower than the average time after one week. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 6.

2-8.q: The management of a tax preparation company has found that there is an 80% cumulative average- time learning curve for the tax preparers. If it takes four hours for a new tax preparer to complete a tax return for the first time, the average hours for the same tax preparer to complete four tax returns are
2-8.e: Estimated cumulative average time per unit = Time required for first unit × LCn Where LC = the learning curve percentage (as a decimal) and n = number of doublings. Four tax returns entails 2 doublings: the first doubling is from 1 to 2, and the second doubling is from 2 to 4. With an 80% cumulative average-time learning curve, the average hours for the same tax preparer to complete four tax returns if the first tax return required four hours is: 4 × 0.802 = 2.56 hours
wrongWhy: This is 4 × 0.80. The estimated cumulative average time per unit is the time required for the first unit multiplied by LCn, where LC is the learning curve percentage and n is the number of doublings. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is 4 × 0.803. The estimated cumulative average time per unit is the time required for the first unit multiplied by LCn, where LC is the learning curve percentage and n is the number of doublings. Four tax returns entails only 2 doublings, not 3: the first doubling is from 1 to 2, and the second doubling is from 2 to 4. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

4 hours is the time required for the first tax return. If the average time required for the same tax preparer to complete four tax returns were 4 hours, that would mean no learning had taken place because each subsequent tax return required the same amount of time as the first one. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 7.

2-8.q: A corporation has determined that when the quantity produced in units doubles from x to 2x, the average time per unit for 2x units is 90% of the average time per unit for x units. The decline in time per unit as production doubles is an example of
2-8.e: This is an example of the cumulative average-time learning model.
wrongWhy: This is not a simple regression cost estimation model.
This is not an example of the incremental unit-time learning model.
This is not the high-low cost estimation model. 8.

2-8.q: A manufacturing company is developing a new product. The first batch of 200 units took 100 direct labor hours to produce. If the company has an 80% learning curve and uses the cumulative average-time learning model, how many hours does it take to produce the third and fourth batches?
2-8.e: The number of direct labor hours required for the third and fourth batches is the difference between the number of hours required for the first four batches and the number of hours required for the first two batches. Step 1: Calculate the estimated time required for batches 1-4: Estimated total direct labor hours = DLH required for the first batch × (2 × LC)n Where LC = Learning curve percentage in decimal format and n = number of doublings. Four batches entails 2 doublings: the first doubling from 1 to 2, and the second doubling from 2 to 4. Estimated total DLH for 4 batches = 100 × (2 × 0.80)2 = 256 hours Step 2: Calculate the estimated total time required for batches 1-2: Two batches entails 1 doubling, from 1 to 2. Estimated total DLH for 2 batches = 100 × (2 × 0.80) = 160 hours Step 3: Calculate the difference between the time required for batches 1-4 and the time required for batches 1-2: Direct labor hours required to produce the third and fourth batches = 256 − 160 = 96 hours.
wrongWhy: 256 hours is the estimated total direct labor hours for the first four batches, but the question asks for the estimated total direct labor hours for only the third and fourth batches. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

160 hours is the estimated total direct labor hours required for the first two batches, but the question asks for the estimated total direct labor hours required for the third and fourth batches. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

200 hours is two times the number of direct labor hours required to produce the first batch. The question asks for the number of direct labor hours it will take to produce the third and fourth batches. If the third and fourth batches require 200 direct labor hours to produce, that would mean no learning had taken place because the third and fourth batches each required the same amount of direct labor hours as the first batch (100 hours each). Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 9.

2-8.q: LCB, Inc. is preparing a bid to the Department of the Navy to produce engines for rescue boats. The company has manufactured these engines for the Navy for the past 3 years, on an exclusive contract, and has experienced the following costs: Cumulative Total Cumulative Units Cumulative Labor Produced Materials Costs 10 $ 60,000 $120,000 20 120,000 192,000 40 240,000 307,200 At LCB, variable overhead is applied on the basis of $1.00 per direct labor dollar. Based on historical costs, LCB knows that the production of 40 engines will be allocated $100,000 of fixed overhead costs. The bid request is for an additional 40 units; all companies submitting bids are allowed to charge a maximum of 25% above full cost for each order. LCB's rate of learning on the 3-year engine contract is
2-8.e: The learning curve percentage (using the cumulative average-time learning model) is the total time or cost actually required for the total production when production doubles divided by the total time or cost if no learning had taken place. It can also be determined by calculating the cumulative average cost per unit at each level and dividing the cumulative average cost per unit at the doubled level by the cumulative average cost at the previous level. In looking at the information provided, we see that the materials costs do not have any efficiencies as production increases. For all levels of production the materials cost is $6,000 per unit. So our attention is focused on the labor. In order to produce 10 units, the company incurred $120,000 of labor. If that same productivity level were to continue (i.e., if no learning were taking place), in order to produce 20 units, they would incur $240,000 of labor costs (2 × $120,000). However, they incurred only $192,000 of labor costs, which is 80% of $240,000 ($192,000 ÷ $240,000). And at a cost of $192,000 for 20 units, if no learning took place as they manufactured the next 20 units, it would have cost a total of $384,000 to produce a total of 40 units, including the first 20. However, it cost only $307,200 for all 40 units, which is 80% of $384,000. So, every time production doubles, the company is experiencing a learning curve of 80%. Calculating it another way, the cumulative average labor cost per unit is $12,000 when a total of 10 units has been produced ($120,000 ÷ 10); and it is $9,600 per unit when 20 units have been produced ($192,000 ÷ 20). $9,600 ÷ $12,000 = 0.80, or an 80% learning curve.
wrongWhy: This is not the correct answer. The learning curve percentage (using the cumulative average-time learning model) is the total time or cost actually required for the total production when production doubles divided by the total time or cost if no learning had taken place. It can also be determined by calculating the cumulative average cost per unit at each level and dividing the cumulative average cost per unit at the doubled level by the cumulative average cost at the previous level. The learning curve percentage (using the cumulative average-time learning model) is the total time or cost actually required for the total production when production doubles divided by the total time or cost if no learning had taken place. It can also be determined by calculating the cumulative average cost per unit at each level and dividing the cumulative average cost per unit at the doubled level by the cumulative average cost at the previous level. This is the cumulative labor cost of $120,000 for 10 units divided by the cumulative labor cost of $192,000 for 20 units; and also the cumulative labor cost of $192,000 for 20 units divided by the cumulative labor cost of $307,200 for 40 units. That is not the correct way to calculate a learning curve. The learning curve percentage (using the cumulative average-time learning model) is the total time or cost actually required for the total production when production doubles divided by the total time or cost if no learning had taken place. It can also be determined by calculating the cumulative average cost per unit at each level and dividing the cumulative average cost per unit at the doubled level by the cumulative average cost at the previous level. 10.

2-8.q: LCB, Inc. is preparing a bid to the Department of the Navy to produce engines for rescue boats. The company has manufactured these engines for the Navy for the past 3 years, on an exclusive contract, and has experienced the following costs: Cumulative Total Cumulative Units Cumulative Labor Produced Materials Costs 10 $ 60,000 $120,000 20 120,000 192,000 40 240,000 307,200 At LCB, variable overhead is applied on the basis of $1.00 per direct labor dollar. Based on historical costs, LCB knows that the production of 40 engines will be allocated $100,000 of fixed overhead costs. The bid request is for an additional 40 units; all companies submitting bids are allowed to charge a maximum of 25% above full cost for each order. The maximum bid price that LCB, Inc. could submit to the Department of the Navy for the 40 units is
2-8.e: The company is able to charge 125% of the full cost for the order. The full cost will include $100,000 of fixed costs and $240,000 of materials costs. In addition, there will be labor costs, However, we need to determine what those labor costs will be. We can calculate that the learning curve is 80%. The first 10 units produced cost $120,000 in labor. If no learning had taken place, the first 20 units would have cost $240,000 in labor. However, the labor cost for the first 20 units was $192,000. By dividing $192,000 by $240,000, we calculate that the learning curve is 80%. We know that labor costs are $307,200 to produce the first 40 units. However, the company experiences an 80% learning curve, which means that the labor cost to produce the next 40 units will not be $307,200, but $184,320. This is calculated as follows: The first 40 units cost $307,200 to produce. If no learning had taken place, it would cost twice that amount, or $614,400 to produce the first 80 units (the first 40 units plus another 40 units). However, as there is a learning curve of 80%, the total cost for the 80 units will be 80% of that $614,400 expected amount, or $491,520. Since the first set of 40 units cost $307,200, the cost of producing only the second set of 40 units was $491,520 − $307,200, which equals $184,320. Variable overhead will also be charged at $1 per direct labor dollar, or $184,320. Therefore, the total costs of production are $708,640 ($100,000 + $240,000 + $184,320 + $184,320). Adding 25% to this, we get $885,800.
wrongWhy: This answer does not include the $100,000 of fixed costs or the 25% markup. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
This answer does not include the markup – it is simply the full cost of production. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
This answer does not include the $100,000 of fixed costs. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. 11.

2-8.q: LCB, Inc. is preparing a bid to the Department of the Navy to produce engines for rescue boats. The company has manufactured these engines for the Navy for the past 3 years, on an exclusive contract, and has experienced the following costs: Cumulative Total Cumulative Units Cumulative Labor Produced Materials Costs 10 $ 60,000 $120,000 20 120,000 192,000 40 240,000 307,200 At LCB, variable overhead is applied on the basis of $1.00 per direct labor dollar. Based on historical costs, LCB knows that the production of 40 engines will be allocated $100,000 of fixed overhead costs. The bid request is for an additional 40 units; all companies submitting bids are allowed to charge a maximum of 25% above full cost for each order. In order to ensure that the company would not lose money on the project, LCB's minimum bid for the 40 units would be
2-8.e: In order to determine the minimum bid, we need to calculate the costs that will be incurred only if the additional 40 engines will be produced. This will include materials of $240,000 (equal to the cost of materials for the 40 units already produced) plus the costs of the labor and variable overhead. We will not include fixed overhead that is allocated to the engines because this overhead would be incurred even if the engines are not produced. To determine the cost of the labor for the next 40 units, we first need to find the learning curve percentage that applies. The first 10 units cost $120,000 in labor. If no learning had taken place, then units 11 through 20 would have also cost $120,000, and the total cumulative labor cost for the first 20 units would have been $240,000. However, the cumulative labor cost for the first 20 units was $192,000. Under the Cumulative Average-Time Learning Model, the formula to find the learning curve would be $120,000 × 2X = $192,000, where X = the learning curve. Solving for X, we get $240,000X = $192,000, and X = 0.80. We can do the same thing for units 21 through 40: $192,000 × 2X = $307,200. $384,000X = $307,200, and X = 0.80. So we know the learning curve is 80%. Therefore, we can calculate the cost of the labor to produce the next 40 engines as follows: $307,200 × (2 × 0.80) = X, where X = the cumulative cost of labor for all 80 units produced. Solving for X, we get X = $491,520. From this, we can calculate the labor cost to produce units 41 through 80. It will be $491,520 minus $307,200 (the cost to produce the first 40 engines), or $184,320. Variable overhead will also be charged at $1 per direct labor dollar, so that will also be $184,320. The variable costs of production are therefore $608,640 ($240,000 direct materials + $184,320 direct labor + $184,320 variable overhead).
wrongWhy: This answer includes the $100,000 of fixed costs that will be allocated to this contract, i.e., "full cost." The question asks for the minimum bid to avoid losing money. Those $100,000 of fixed costs will be paid by the company as a whole whether or not the company gets this contract. The only costs that should be included in this analysis are incremental costs that would be incurred only if the production of the additional 40 engines takes place. Since the $100,000 in allocated fixed cost is not an incremental cost, it should not be included.
This is 25% above full cost, which is the maximum the company would be allowed to charge. However, the question asks for the minimum bid to avoid losing money.
This is 25% above variable costs. The question asks for the minimum bid to avoid losing money. This is not the minimum bid to avoid losing money. 12.

2-8.q: The average labor cost per unit for the first batch produced by a new process is $120. The cumulative average labor cost after the second batch is $72 per product. Using a batch size of 100 and assuming the learning curve continues, the total labor cost of four batches will be
2-8.e: With the cumulative average time learning model, whenever the total quantity of units produced doubles, the cumulative average time per unit required for all the units produced is X% of the cumulative average time per unit required at the previous production-doubling level, where X% represents the learning rate. This question gives labor costs instead of labor time; however, since the question also asks for total labor cost, the costs can be used in the same way as time would be used. The question says that the average labor cost for the first batch is $120 per unit and the cumulative average labor cost after the second batch (the first doubling) is $72 per unit. Using this information, the learning rate is calculated at 60% (72 ÷ 120 = 0.60). If the average cost per unit for the first two batches is $72, then the average cost per unit for four batches (the second doubling) is $72 × 0.6, or $43.20. Since each batch contains 100 units, 4 batches contain a total of 400 units. If the average cost per unit is $43.20, the total cost for 400 units (4 batches) is $43.20 × 400, or $17,280.
wrongWhy: This is the average cost of one unit after 800 units have been produced multiplied by 400. The average cost of one unit after 400 units have been produced needs to be multiplied by 400 to find the total labor cost of four batches of 100.

This is the average cost of one unit after 400 units have been produced multiplied by 100. The average cost of one unit after 400 units have been produced needs to be multiplied by 400 to find the total labor cost of four batches of 100.

This is the average cost of one unit after 800 units have been produced multiplied by 100. The average cost of one unit after 400 units have been produced needs to be multiplied by 400 to find the total labor cost of four batches of 100. 13.

2-8.q: Management of a bookkeeping company observed that the average time spent to perform identical tasks using a new software package decreases as the number of tasks performed increases. The following information on the use of the new software was collected. Number of Tasks Total Time to Average Time Performed Perform All Tasks To Perform Each Task 1 10 minutes 10 minutes 2 18 minutes 9 minutes 4 32.4 minutes 8.1 minutes If this learning effect continues, what is the average time to perform each of the first eight tasks?
2-8.e: The learning curve for each doubling of production is 90%. This is calculated using the following formula for the Cumulative Average-Time Learning Model: Initial time × 2 × Learning Curve = Time for Doubled Work Using the numbers given, and letting the learning curve be X: 10 × 2 × X = 18 20X = 18 X = 0.90 And, 18 × 2 × X = 32.4 36X = 32.4 X = 0.90 We can also use the average time per task: 10 × X = 9 X = 0.9 And, 9 × X = 8.1 X = 0.9 Therefore, the average time to complete 8 units will be 90% of the average time to complete 4 units. It took 8.1 minutes on average to complete the each of the first four tasks, so it will take 7.29 minutes on average to complete each of the first 8 tasks (8.1 × 0.9).
wrongWhy: This is the amount of time, on average, it will take to perform the first 32 tasks.
This is the amount of time, on average, it took to perform the first four tasks.
This is the amount of time, on average, it will take to perform the first 16 tasks. 14.

2-8.q: Lake Corporation manufactures specialty components for the electronics industry in a highly labor intensive environment. Arc Electronics has asked Lake to bid on a component that Lake made for Arc last month. The previous order was for 80 units and required 120 hours of direct labor to manufacture. Arc would now like 240 additional components. Lake experiences an 80% learning curve on all of its jobs. The number of direct labor hours needed for Lake to complete the 240 additional components is
2-8.e: With an 80% learning curve it means that each time production doubles, the number of hours required for the total production (including the units already produced) will be 80% less than would be expected given the hours required for the previous production. Therefore, producing a total of 160 units will take 192 hours (120 × 2 × 0.8). Based on the fact that the first 80 units took 120 hours, without any learning, we would expect that production of 160 units would take 240 hours. However, it took only 80% of that time, or 192 hours. Given that 160 units take 192 hours, we would expect without any learning that 320 units would take 384 hours. However, it will take only 80% of that time because of the learning curve. This means it will take 307.2 hours to produce 320 units. Since the question asks how many hours will be needed to produce the additional 240 units, we need to subtract the time required to produce the first 80 units (120 hours) from the time required to produce all 320 units (307.2 hours). This means that it will take 187.2 hours to produce the additional 240 units.
wrongWhy: This is the amount of time it will take to produce 320 units, not the amount of time it will take to produce the additional 240 units. Since the question asks how many hours will be needed to produce the additional 240 units, we need to subtract the time required to produce the first 80 units from the time required to produce all 320 units.

This answer does not take into consideration the learning curve and the improvements that come with it. This is the number of direct labor hours that would be required to manufacture 240 units if no learning at all took place. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

This is 80 × 2 × 2 × 0.8. This is incorrect for three reasons. (1) 80 is the number of units manufactured in the first order. It is not the number of hours of direct labor required to manufacture those 80 units, and the number of hours of direct labor is what should be used. (2) One multiplication by 0.8 is missing in the calculation. The number of hours required for the first order of 80 units should be multiplied by 2 × 0.8 × 2 × 0.8 to find the number of hours required for units 1 through 320. But that number of hours is still not the answer to the problem. (3) The number of hours required for the first 80 units needs to be subtracted from the number of hours calculated in (2) to find the number of hours required for only units 81 through 320, because those units represent the new order for 240 units. 15.

2-8.q: Aerosub Inc. has developed a new product for spacecraft that includes the manufacturing of a complex part. The manufacturing of this part requires a high degree of technical skill. Management believes there is a good opportunity for its technical force to learn and improve as they become accustomed to the production process. The production of the first unit requires 10,000 direct labor hours. If an 80% learning curve is used and eight units are produced, the cumulative average direct labor hours required per unit of the product will be
2-8.e: The total number of direct labor hours required to produce 8 units is: 10,000 (2 × 0.80) (2 × 0.80) (2 × 0.80), or 10,000 (2 × 0.80)3, which equals 40,960 hours. The cumulative average direct labor hours required per unit of the product will be 40,960 ÷ 8, or 5,120 hours. The average number of hours per unit under the Cumulative Average-Time Learning Model can also be calculated by multiplying the time required for the first unit by the learning curve percentage raised to the appropriate exponent. For this problem, the calculation would be 10,000 × 0.803 = 5,120.
wrongWhy: 8,000 hours is the amount of time required to produce the first unit (10,000 hours) multiplied by 0.8. This does not result in the cumulative average direct labor hours required per unit for production of 8 units.

10,000 hours is the amount of time required to produce the first unit. The subsequent units produced require less time. Therefore, the cumulative average direct labor hours required per unit of the product will be less than 10,000.

6,400 hours is the amount of time required to produce the first unit multiplied by 0.82. This does not result in the cumulative average direct labor hours required per unit for production of 8 units. 16.

2-8.q: Aerosub Inc. has developed a new product for spacecraft that includes the manufacturing of a complex part. The manufacturing of this part requires a high degree of technical skill. Management believes there is a good opportunity for its technical force to learn and improve as they become accustomed to the production process. The production of the first unit requires 10,000 direct labor hours. If an 80% learning curve is used, the cumulative direct labor hours required for producing a total of eight units would be
2-8.e: The total number of direct labor hours required to produce 8 units using the Cumulative Average- Time Learning Model is: 10,000 (2 × 0.80) (2 × 0.80) (2 × 0.80), or 10,000 (2 × 0.80)3, which equals 40,960 hours.
wrongWhy: This is the number of direct labor hours that would be required if no learning took place.

This is 80% of the total number of hours that would be required to produce 8 units if no learning took place. This is not the way to calculate the cumulative direct labor hours required for producing a total of eight units.

This answer results from multiplying $10,000 by 0.8 and getting $8,000; then multiplying $8,000 by 0.8 and getting $6,400; then multiplying $6,400 by 0.8 and getting $5,120; then summing $10,000 + $8,000 + $6,400 + $5,120 = $29,520. This is not the correct way to calculate the cumulative direct labor hours required for producing a total of eight units. 17.

2-8.q: Seacraft Inc. received a request for a competitive bid for the sale of one of its unique boating products with a desired modification. Seacraft is now in the process of manufacturing this product but with a slightly different modification for another customer. These unique products are labor intensive and both will have long production runs. Which one of the following methods should Seacraft use to estimate the cost of the new competitive bid?
2-8.e: Learning curve analysis states that the more experience people have with doing a task, the more efficient they become in doing that task. As production involves intensive labor usage, learning curve analysis is appropriate to use to use to estimate the cost of the new competitive bid. Seacraft is placing a bid to produce a customized, unique product. The company has had previous experience in producing a similar product for another customer. Because of Seacraft's previous experience, its costs to produce a similar product will be less time consuming and consequently lower than if the company had never produced this type of product before. Seacraft can therefore set its bid lower because of its anticipated lower costs.
wrongWhy: Expected value analysis is an effective method to use in conditions of risk. It is calculated by multiplying each possible outcome by related probability added up together. In other words it is weighted average of possible outcomes. It is used to select the best of given alternatives. Expected value analysis is not the best method for Seacraft to use to estimate the cost of the new competitive bid.

Regression analysis is a quantitative method that is used to find an equation for the linear relationships between or among variables. That result can be used to draw conclusions and make forecasts. To use regression analysis, historical data is required for the variable that we are forecasting or for the variables that are causal to this variable. If historical data is not available, regression analysis cannot be used. Even when historical data is available, if there has been a significant change in the conditions surrounding that data, its use is questionable for predicting the future. Regression analysis is not the best method for Seacraft to use to estimate the cost of the new competitive bid.

Simulation is a process of changing key variables to determine the possible change in the optimal solution because of changes in the variables. It is used to define how sensitive the project (sales for example) is to a change in those variables. Continuous probability simulation is not the best method for Seacraft to use to estimate the cost of the new competitive bid. 18.

2-8.q: A manufacturing company notices that when its cumulative production doubles, it observes a 10% decrease in the average time it has taken to produce one unit of product, compared with the average time per unit before the doubling. Based on a cumulative average-time learning model, this decrease in unit production time implies a learning curve of
2-8.e: Under the cumulative average-time learning model, each time the total number of units produced doubles, the total production time for all the units produced decreases to an amount equal to two times the last total production time, multiplied by the learning percentage. If the average time for all the units produced has decreased by 10% when the cumulative production has doubled one time, the learning curve percentage is 90% (100% − 10%). Furthermore, when the cumulative average-time learning model is being used, the learning rate will always be greater than 50% and less than 100%. Among the answer choices given, only this answer choice fulfills that requirement.
wrongWhy: When the cumulative average-time learning model is being used, the learning rate will always be greater than 50% and less than 100%. A learning rate of 5% is not a possibility. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

When the cumulative average-time learning model is being used, the learning rate will always be greater than 50% and less than 100%. A learning rate of 110% is not a possibility. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

When the cumulative average-time learning model is being used, the learning rate will always be greater than 50% and less than 100%. A learning rate of 10% is not a possibility. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 19.

2-8.q: Moss Point Manufacturing recently completed and sold an order of 50 units that had costs as follows. $15,400 *Applied on the basis of direct labor hours. **Applied at the rate of 10% of variable cost. The company has now been requested to prepare a bid for 150 units of the same product. If Moss Point had experienced a 70% learning curve, the bid for the 150 units would
o: Include increased fixed overhead cost per unit
Include 6.40 direct labor hours per unit at $8.50 per hour
Show a 30% reduction in the total direct labor hours required with no learning curve
Be 10% lower than the total bid at an 80% learning curve
2-8.e: Since there are two doublings, the number of hours required for 200 units using a 70% learning curve is: 1,000 hours × (0.7 × 2) × (0.7 × 2) = 1,960 hours. 1,960 hours required for 200 units less 1,000 hours required for the first 50 units = 960 hours required for the last 150 units. 960 hours ÷ 150 units = an average of 6.4 hours required per unit for the last 150 units.
wrongWhy: Fixed overhead cost per unit would decrease, not increase, since the cost is applied at a rate based on variable cost. Variable cost includes direct materials, direct labor, and variable overhead. Variable overhead is applied on the basis of direct labor hours. Since direct labor hours required per unit will decrease because of the learning curve, both direct labor cost per unit and variable overhead cost per unit will decrease. As a result, fixed overhead cost per unit will also decrease.

If there were no learning curve the total direct labor hours would be approximately 4,000 hours. The number of hours required for the full 200 units with a 70% learning curve is 1,960 (1,000 hours × [0.7 × 2] × [0.7 × 2] = 1,960 hours). The number of hours required for the 150 units is 1,960 minus the 1,000 hours required for the first 50 units, or 960 hours. The number of hours required for the 150 units if no learning had taken place would be 3,000 (4,000 minus 1,000). That would be a reduction of 2,040 hours (3,000 minus 960), a 68% reduction (2,040 ÷ 3,000).

If there were an 80% learning curve instead of 70%, the bid would need to be higher (not lower) than it would be at a 70% learning curve, because the number of direct labor hours required for the 150 units would be greater. 20.

2-8.q: A manufacturing company has the opportunity to submit a bid for 20 units of a product on which it has already produced two 10-unit lots. The production manager believes that the learning experience observed on the first two lots will continue for at least the next two lots. The direct labor required on the first two lots was as follows. 5,000 direct labor hours for the first lot of 10 units 3,000 additional direct labor hours for the second lot of 10 units The learning rate experienced by the company on the first two lots of this product using the Cumulative Average-Time Learning Model is
2-8.e: The time required to produce the first lot was 5,000 direct labor hours. The total time required to produce the first two lots was 8,000 direct labor hours. If no learning had occurred, the total time required to produce the first two lots would have been 5,000 × 2, or 10,000 direct labor hours. The actual time required divided by the time that would have been required if no learning had taken place is the learning rate under the Cumulative Average-Time Learning Model. 8,000 ÷ 10,000 = 0.80, so the learning rate on the first two lots was 80%.
wrongWhy: This is 3,000 divided by 5,000. That is not the way to calculate the learning curve. The actual total time required for all the units produced in both lots divided by the time that would have been required if no learning had taken place is the learning rate under the Cumulative Average-Time Learning Model.

This is the rate of decrease in the time required for the second lot. That is not the way to calculate the learning rate. When the Cumulative Average-Time Learning Model is being used, the learning curve must be less than 100% and greater than 50%. A learning curve of 50% or less is not possible.

This is the amount of time required for the first lot divided by the total amount of time required for the first two lots. That is not the way to calculate the learning rate. The actual total time required for all the units produced in both lots divided by the time that would have been required if no learning had taken place is the learning rate under the Cumulative Average-Time Learning Model. 21.

2-8.q: Langley Corporation is developing a new product that will be manufactured in pairs. The company recently produced the first two units of this product using 200 hours of direct labor time. 2-9.q: Ryotel is conducting market research to determine whether or not to launch a new product. The expected value of the new product's annual profit is
2-9.e: The expected value of the new product's annual profit is a weighted average of the expected value of profits with favorable research results and the expected value of profits with unfavorable research results. The expected value of profits with favorable research results is [0.70 × $10,000,000] + [0.30 × $(4,000,000)] = $5,800,000. The probability that the research results will be favorable is 60%. The expected value of profits with unfavorable research results is [0.25 × $10,000,000] + [0.75 × $(4,000,000)] = $(500,000). The probability that the research results will be unfavorable is 40%. The expected value of the new product's annual profit is [0.60 × $5,800,000] + [0.40 × $(500,000)] = $3,280,000.

2-9.q: The Lions Club is planning to sell pretzels at a local football game and has estimated sales demand as follows. The cost of the pretzels varies with the quantity purchased as follows. Any unsold pretzels would be donated to the local food bank. The calculated profits at the various sales demand levels and purchase quantities are as follows. Expected Profits at Various Purchase Quantity Levels 8,000 $6,000 $4,000 $ 2,200 $ (500) 10,000 6,000 8,000 6,200 3,500 12,000 6,000 8,000 10,200 7,500 15,000 6,000 8,000 10,200 13,500 Which of the following purchase quantities would you recommend to the Lions Club?
2-9.e: We need to find the expected profit at each purchase quantity level in order to determine the highest expected profit. The information on the cost of the pretzels at each purchase quantity is irrelevant, because the table gives the expected profits at the various purchase quantity and sales demand levels, and profit has already been reduced by the cost of sales. The probability of each level of sales demand, though, is relevant. That is used as the weighting in calculating the expected value of the profit at each purchase quantity level. The expected profit for the purchase quantity level of 8,000 is $6,000 and needs no calculation, since the expected profit is $6,000 at every level of sales demand. The expected profit at a purchase quantity of 10,000 is (0.10 × $4,000) + (0.90 × $8,000) = $7,600. At a purchase quantity of 12,000, the expected profit is (0.10 × $2,200) + (0.40 × $6,200) + (0.50 × $10,200) = $7,800. At a purchase quantity of 15,000, the expected profit is (0.10 × [$500]) + (0.40 × $3,500) + (0.30 × $7,500) + (0.20 × $13,500) = $6,300. The highest expected profit is $7,800 at a purchase quantity of 12,000. So a purchase quantity of 12,000 should be recommended.
wrongWhy: This is the sales demand level that has the highest probability of occurring (40%); but this is not the purchase quantity that would result in the highest expected profit for the Lions Club. Therefore, it is not the purchase quantity that should be recommended.

The Lions Club would have a profit of $6,000 if they purchase 8,000 pretzels, regardless of what sales demand is. However, $6,000 is not the highest expected profit. Therefore, purchasing 8,000 pretzels would not be the best recommendation.

If the Lions Club purchases 15,000 pretzels and if sales demand is also 15,000 pretzels, their profit will be the highest at this level ($13,500). However, the club has no guarantee that sales demand will be 15,000; so purchasing 15,000 pretzels would not be the best recommendation. 4.

2-9.q: According to recent focus sessions, Norton Corporation has a "can't miss" consumer product on its hands. Sales forecasts indicate either excellent or good results, with Norton's sales manager assigning a probability of .6 to a good results outcome. The company is now studying various sales compensation plans for the product and has determined the following contribution margin data. Contribution Margin If sales are excellent and If sales are good and On the basis of this information, which of the following statements is correct?
o: Plan 1 should be adopted because it is $8,000 more attractive than Plan 2
Plan 1 should be adopted because of the sales manager's higher confidence in good results
Plan 2 should be adopted because it is $10,000 more attractive than Plan 1
Either Plan should be adopted, the decision being dependent on the probability of excellent sales results
2-9.e: This question requires the calculation of the expected value of choosing Compensation Plan 1 and the expected value of choosing Compensation Plan 2, and then selecting the plan with the higher expected value. The expected value of each compensation plan is a weighted average of the possible contribution margins that would result under it, using the probabilities of the sales results as the weights. The contribution margin is total revenue minus total variable expenses. Regardless of which compensation plan is chosen, there is a 60% probability that sales will be good. Since sales will be either good or excellent, then there must be a 40% probability (100% − 60%) that sales will be excellent. Under Plan 1, the contribution margin will be $300,000 if sales are excellent (40% probability) and $240,000 if sales are good (60% probability). So the expected value of choosing Plan 1 is ($300,000 × 0.40) + ($240,000 × 0.60), which is $264,000. Under Plan 2, the contribution margin will be $370,000 if sales are excellent (40% probability) and $180,000 if sales are good (60% probability). So the expected value of choosing Plan 2 is ($370,000 × 0.40) + ($180,000 × 0.60), or $256,000. The expected value of Plan 1, $264,000, is $8,000 greater than the expected value of Plan 2, $256,000, so Plan 1 should be adopted.
wrongWhy: The plan that should be adopted is the one with the higher expected value.

This answer results from summing the two potential contribution margins if Plan 1 is adopted ($300,000 if sales are excellent + $240,000 if sales are good = $540,000), then summing the two potential contribution margins if Plan 2 is adopted ($370,000 if sales are excellent + $180,000 if sales are good = $550,000), finding the difference between the two sums, and selecting Plan 2 because the sum of its two contribution margins is $10,000 greater than the sum of the two contribution margins for Plan 1. The potential contribution margins are mutually exclusive. Norton Corporation will not earn both $300,000 and $240,000 if Plan 1 is adopted, and it will not earn both $370,000 and $180,000 if Plan 2 is adopted. There is a 60% probability that sales will be good and therefore there is a 40% probability (100% - 60%) that sales will be excellent. Those probabilities need to be used to find the expected values of Plan 1 and Plan 2. Then find the difference between the two expected values and select the plan with the higher expected value.
The plan that should be adopted is the one with the higher expected value. 8.

2-9.q: A clothing company is evaluating two possible locations for a new retail store. The company’s research indicates that location A has a 45% probability of generating $400,000 in cash flow, and a 55% probability of generating $325,000 in cash flow. Location B has a 25% probability of generating $600,000 in cash flow, and a 75% probability of generating $250,000 in cash flow. o: B because its expected value is $125,000 higher than location A
B because its expected value is $146,250 higher than location A
A because its expected value is $21,250 higher than location B
A because its expected value is $75,000 higher than location B
2-9.e: The expected value of the cash flows for each location is calculated as the weighted average of the possible values of the location's cash flows using the probabilities of each of the outcomes as the weights. The expected value of A's cash flow is calculated as: (0.45 × $400,000) + (0.55 × $325,000) = $358,750 The expected value of B's cash flow is calculated as: (0.25 × $600,000) + (0.75 × $250,000) = $337,500. $358,750 − $337,500 = $21,250. The company should choose location A because the expected value of its cash flow is $21,250 higher than the expected value of location B's cash flow. The expected value is an average of all the possible outcomes that could occur over a long period of time. An expected value will probably not be equal to the actual outcome in any specific period; but over a long period of time, it should approximate the average of the actual outcomes.
wrongWhy: This answer is the difference between the sum of the two potential cash flows for location B and the sum of the two potential cash flows for location A. The expected value of the cash flows for each location is calculated as the weighted average of the possible values of the location's cash flows using the probabilities of each of the outcomes as the weights. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is the probability of A's cash flow being $400,000 (45%) multiplied by A's cash flow that has a probability of 55% ($325,000). The expected value of the cash flows for each location is calculated as the weighted average of the possible values of the location's cash flows using the probabilities of each of the outcomes as the weights. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is the difference between the lower projected value for A and the lower projected value for B. The expected value of the cash flows for each location is calculated as the weighted average of the possible values of the location's cash flows using the probabilities of each of the outcomes as the weights. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 9.
