wrongWhy: There are a number of methods of developing budgets: participative budgeting, bottom-up budgeting, top-down approach, etc. Any level of management can establish either a flexible budget or a static budget or both.
Flexible budgets and static budgets both include both variable and fixed costs.
A flexible budget and a static budget are usually both prepared for planning and performance evaluation purposes. 20.

2-10.q: The type of budget that is continually updated to add a new budget period as the most recent budget period is completed is called a(n)
2-10.e: A rolling budget is a budget that is prepared for a certain number of periods, for example six months, into the future. And as a month is completed, another month is added in the future at the end of the budget. For example, a company with a six-month rolling budget will have a budget for January through June, and at the end of January, they will prepare the budget for July. Thus, they will always have a budget for six months into the future. That is what has been described in this question.
wrongWhy: This question does not describe zero-based budgeting.
This question does not describe activity based budgeting.
This question does not describe flexible budgeting. 21.

2-10.q: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is called a
2-10.e: The master budget expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year. The master budget is a full set of budgeted financial statements for the budget year, including monthly or at least quarterly interim budgeted financial statements. The budgeted financial statements include the budgeted balance sheet, budgeted income statement, and budgeted statement of cash flows. The master budget is created using both non-financial and financial assumptions, which come about as a result of the planning process. The master budget is a result of both operating decisions and financing decisions. Operating decisions are concerned with the best use of the company’s limited resources. Financing decisions are concerned with obtaining the funds to acquire the resources the company needs. The master budget is a static budget because each item in it is developed using an assumption that includes just one projected activity level.
wrongWhy: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a flexible budget. A flexible budget is based on the master budget, but it adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity supporting those amounts had been used in preparing the budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a strategic budget. A strategic budget is a budget that is aligned with and takes into consideration the entity's long term objectives that will take more than one year to achieve. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a rolling budget. A rolling budget is a budget that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous, or rolling, budget would be prepared at the end of every month for the next twelve months. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 22.

2-10.q: A systemized approach known as zero-base budgeting (ZBB)
o: Presents a statement of expectations for a period of time but does not present a firm commitment
Presents the plan for only one level of activity and does not adjust to changes in the level of activity
Divides the activities of individual responsibility centers into a series of packages that are prioritized
Focuses on the budgeted cost of activities necessary to produce products
2-10.e: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. This is quite different from a budget that is prepared by looking at the current year's actual or budgeted amounts and simply adjusting them (usually increasing them) for expected changes in the coming year. Though this method is more time consuming and difficult for all of the people involved, there are a number of advantages to the company as a result of using this method. Because the budget is built up from zero, each manager must justify all of the expenses in his or her department. Each component is evaluated from a cost-benefit perspective and priorities are determined. Zero-based budgeting enables the company to identify expenses that are not value adding or that should be reduced due to some development in production methods or something similar.
wrongWhy: Zero-based budgeting represents a firm commitment to the company just like any other budget.

This is a description of a static budget, not a zero-base budget. A static budget is a budget that is prepared for only one level of activity within the company. A zero-base budget may be a static budget, but it may also be a flexible budget.
Zero-based budgeting is applicable to all areas of the organization and includes all selling and administrative costs, not only costs for activities to produce products. 23.

2-10.q: In an organization that plans by using comprehensive budgeting, the master budget refers to:
o: the current budget updated for operations for part of the current year
the booklet containing budget guidelines, policies, and forms to use in the budgeting process
a budget of a not-for-profit organization after it is approved by the appropriate authoritative body
a compilation of all the separate operational and financial budget schedules of the organization
2-10.e: The individual budgets that make up the operating and financial budgets are compiled into a budgeted income statement, balance sheet, and statement of cash flows, which is the master budget.
wrongWhy: This does not describe the master budget.
This does not describe the master budget.
This does not describe the master budget. 24.

2-10.q: An advantage of incremental budgeting when compared with zero-base budgeting is that incremental budgeting
o: Eliminates functions and duties that have outlived their usefulness
Accepts the existing base as being satisfactory
Eliminates the need to review all functions periodically to obtain optimum use of resources
2-10.e: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year's budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget or actual amounts. Incremental budgeting assumes that the previous period's budgeted or actual results are satisfactory, and the budget is calculated by adjusting the previous period budgeted or actual amount by a number, for example 1.1, to allow for changes planned for the new budgeting period. Thus, it is easier to prepare an incremental budget and less managerial effort is consumed than when the budget is prepared under the ZBB concept.
wrongWhy: This is an advantage of zero-based budgeting, not incremental budgeting.
There is no difference of new project treatment under both of these budget development approaches.
Periodic review of business functions is required regardless the type of budget development approach used. 25.

2-10.q: Which one of the following types of budgets will allow management to best assess how costs will change based on changes in cost drivers?
2-10.e: An activity-based budget allows management to assess how costs will change based on changes in cost drivers. Cost drivers are activities performed that cause costs to be incurred. Those activities could be direct labor hours or machine hours, but they could also be something like number of parts to be used in assembling a product or number of machine set-ups to be performed for the budgeted production volume. Activity-based budgeting is used with activity- based costing.
wrongWhy: A cost budget is not a budget that allows management to assess how costs will change based on changes in cost drivers. There is no specific budget named "cost budget," but any budget that entails costs could be considered a "cost budget." Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A production budget is not a budget that allows management to assess how costs will change based on changes in cost drivers. The production budget is part of the master budget development. The production budget incorporates the projection of how many units will need to be produced during the period, using the projected sales in the company's sales budget along with its capacity and its objectives for beginning and ending inventories. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A rolling budget is not a budget that allows management to assess how costs will change based on changes in cost drivers. A rolling budget is a budget that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous, or rolling, budget would be prepared at the end of every month for the next twelve months. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 26.

2-10.q: Country Ovens is a family restaurant chain. Due to an unexpected road construction project, traffic passing by the Country Ovens restaurant in Newtown has significantly increased. As a result, restaurant volume has similarly increased well beyond the level expected. Which type of budget would be most appropriate in helping the restaurant manager plan for restaurant labor costs?
2-10.e: A flexible budget would be the most appropriate budget, as it would allow management to measure operating success at any activity level experienced. A flexible budget can be adjusted upward for increased traffic and, if necessary, adjusted back down when the construction is completed, providing management with accurate budget variance information under a variety of circumstances.
wrongWhy: Activity-based budgeting is similar in concept to activity-based costing. Activities that drive the costs are identified, a budgeted level of activity for each of these drivers is determined based on a budgeted level of production, and budgeted amounts are developed based on the budgeted level of activity. This is a good basis for developing a budget, but since it depends upon a budgeted level of activity, the budget developed will be a static budget that will not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation.

Zero-based budgeting is a budgeting method in which the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget and the likely operating results for the current period. Although this approach would be appropriate when the next year's volume is expected to far exceed this year’s sales volume, it is still a static budget. And a static budget does not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation.

A rolling budget is a continual budget that adds one month as another month is completed. This type of budget is a static budget, and a static budget does not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation. 27.

2-10.q: The use of the master budget throughout the year as a constant comparison with actual results signifies that the master budget is also a
2-10.e: This is a description of a static budget because the static budget is prepared for only one level of activity (a certain level of sales).
wrongWhy: A flexible budget is prepared for the actual level of activity, so it is not constant throughout the year.

The capital budget is the budget in which all capital (property, plant and equipment) expenditures are planned. The capital budget is often prepared for several years in advance. The amounts in it that affect the current year's budget will be included in the current year's master budget; but the master budget is not also the capital budget.

In a zero-base budget the current year's budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. Not all master budgets are zero-base budgets, and the fact that the master budget is used to make comparisons with actual results does not mean that it must be a zero-based budget. 28.

2-10.q: A company prepared a master budget based on 100 budgeted sales units with a $100 sales price per unit, a variable cost per unit of $50, and $2,000 in total fixed cost. The actual sales quantity was 70 units. When preparing a flexible budget, the operating income is
2-10.e: The operating income for a flexible budget can be calculated by multiplying the contribution per unit by the number of units that were actually sold and then subtracting the fixed costs. In this question, the contribution per unit is $50. This is calculated as the $100 sales price minus the $50 variable cost per unit. Multiplying this $50 per unit by the 70 units were sold gives us a contribution of $3,500. We then need to subtract the $2,000 in fixed costs and we get the operating income for selling 70 units. That is $1,500.
wrongWhy: This is the expected total contribution for a sales level of 70 units.
This is the expected operating income for a sales level of 100 units.
This is the expected total contribution for a sales level of 100 units. 29.

2-10.q: A company reported the following cost information for the last fiscal year when it produced 100,000 units. All costs are variable except for $100,000 of manufacturing overhead and $100,000 of selling and administrative expenses. Using flexible budgeting, what are the total costs associated with producing and selling 110,000 units?
2-10.e: Assuming all variable costs per unit and all fixed costs in total will be the same for the budget period as they were for the last fiscal year, the flexible budget total costs will be: Direct labor ($200,000 ÷ 100,000) × 110,000 $220,000 Direct materials ($100,000 ÷ 100,000) × 110,000 110,000 Variable manufacturing overhead ($100,000 ÷ 100,000) × 110,000 110,000 Variable selling and administrative ($50,000 ÷ 100,000) × 110,000 55,000 Fixed manufacturing overhead 100,000 Fixed selling and administrative 100,000 Total costs $695,000
wrongWhy: $715,000 is the total costs reported for the last fiscal year when the company produced 100,000 units, divided by 100,000 and the result multiplied by 110,000. However, some of those costs were fixed costs. Fixed costs do not change with changes in the activity level, as long as the volume remains within the relevant range. Since the question does not state that a volume of 110,000 units is outside the relevant range (and does not give an increased total fixed cost figure for producing and selling 110,000 units), the only possible assumption is that fixed costs will not change in total. Therefore, it is not appropriate to increase the fixed costs for the increase in production and sales expected for the budget period.

$450,000 is the variable cost to produce and sell 100,000 units. Total cost includes fixed costs as well as variable costs; and the flexible budget cost should be based on 110,000 units, not 100,000 units. Furthermore, the total cost to produce and sell 100,000 units during the last fiscal year was $650,000, and the cost to produce and sell 110,000 units during the budget period would not be $200,000 less than the cost to produce and sell 100,000 units.

$650,000 is the total costs reported for the last fiscal year when the company produced 100,000 units. The company plans to produce and sell 110,000 units during the budget period, and variable expenses for 110,000 units will be greater in total than they were for 100,000 units. 30.

2-10.q: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is a
2-10.e: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is a rolling budget. A rolling budget is a budget that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous, or rolling, budget would be prepared at the end of every month for the next twelve months.
wrongWhy: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is not a sales budget. The sales budget is the first operating budget to be prepared in the process of developing the master budget for the year. It incorporates the expected sales in units of each product and each product’s expected selling price. The sales budget is based on the firm’s forecasted sales level, its short- and long-term objectives, and its production capacity. Since it is part of the development of the master budget, the sales budget is developed using just one projected sales level. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is not a flexible budget. A flexible budget is a budget in which the master budget amounts have been adjusted to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity supporting those amounts had been used in preparing the budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is not a master budget. A master budget is a budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year, and each item in it is developed using an assumption that includes just one projected activity level. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 31.

2-10.q: The estimated unit costs for a company using absorption (full) costing and planning to produce and sell at a level of 12,000 units per month are as follows. Cost Item Estimated Unit Cost Estimated total costs that would be incurred during a month with a production level of 12,000 units and a sales level of 8,000 units are:
2-10.e: Total estimated costs include both production and nonproduction (here, selling) costs and both variable and fixed costs. Total estimated manufacturing costs include manufacturing costs for the total number of units planned to be produced, not sold. Total estimated costs also include the fixed and variable selling costs that are expensed as incurred, but for selling costs, the total number of units planned to be sold is used, not the number of units planned to be produced. Total estimated fixed manufacturing overhead must be calculated by multiplying the estimated fixed manufacturing overhead per unit by the number of units planned to be produced per month. The estimated total fixed manufacturing overhead was determined before the estimated unit cost was determined in the planning process, and it does not change in total with changes in production level. The fixed manufacturing overhead per unit was then determined by dividing the total estimated fixed manufacturing overhead by the number of units planned to be produced. So to find the estimated total fixed manufacturing overhead, multiply the estimated fixed manufacturing overhead per unit ($6) by the number of units planned to be produced (12,000). Estimated total costs are: Variable manufacturing cost of $67 per unit in total × 12,000 units $804,000 planned to be produced: Fixed manufacturing overhead of $6 per unit × 12,000 units planned to 72,000 be produced: Variable selling expense of $3 per unit × 8,000 units sold: 24,000 Fixed selling expense (12,000 planned sales × the estimated fixed selling 48,000 cost of $4/unit): $948,000 Note that total fixed selling expense does not change if only 8,000 units are sold instead of 12,000. That is true because fixed expenses are fixed—they do not change in total with changes in the level of activity.
wrongWhy: This answer results from multiplying the total estimated unit costs ($80 in total) by 12,000 units. The estimated variable selling cost per unit should be multiplied by the 8,000 units sold. See the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from multiplying the estimated per unit variable manufacturing costs ($67 in total for direct materials, direct labor, and variable manufacturing overhead) by the 8,000 units sold and multiplying all the other estimated unit costs by 12,000 units. The variable and fixed manufacturing costs should be multiplied by 12,000 units. The estimated variable selling cost per unit should be multiplied by the 8,000 units sold. The fixed selling costs should be multiplied by the 12,000 units planned to be sold because total fixed selling expense does not change if only 8,000 units are sold instead of the planned 12,000 units. See the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from multiplying the estimated per unit total manufacturing costs ($73 in total for direct materials, direct labor, variable manufacturing overhead, and fixed manufacturing overhead) by 12,000 units and multiplying the estimated per unit selling costs ($7 for variable and fixed selling costs) by the 8,000 units sold. The estimated fixed selling cost per unit should be multiplied by the 12,000 units planned to be sold because total fixed selling expense does not change if only 8,000 units are sold instead of the planned 12,000 units. See the correct answer explanation for more information. 32.

2-10.q: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is a(n)
2-10.e: A flexible budget takes the variable revenues and costs as they are planned in the master budget (the static budget) and adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity level had been used in preparing the budget. Thus, a flexible budget is developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period.
wrongWhy: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not an operating budget. Operating budgets are used to identify the resources that will be needed to carry out the planned activities during the budget period, such as sales, services, production, purchasing, marketing, and R&D (research and development). The operating budgets for individual units are compiled into the budgeted income statement. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not a continuous budget. A continuous budget, also called a rolling budget, is one that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous budget would be prepared at the end of every month or possibly quarter for the next twelve months. Each month or quarter, the month or quarter just completed is dropped from the budget and a new monthly or quarterly budget is added to the end of the budget. At the same time as a month or quarter is dropped and a new month or quarter is added, the other periods in the budget can be revised to reflect any new information that has become available. Thus, the budget is continuously being updated and always covers the same amount of time in the future. For example, in September 20X3, a monthly rolling budget will cover the months of October 20X3 through September 20X4. In October 20X3, the rolling budget will cover the months of November 20X3 through October 20X4. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not a life-cycle budget. A life-cycle budget is used to plan incomes and expenses for one specific product throughout its entire life cycle, from its development through its decline, enabling the company to see the cash flows that will result from the product over its entire life. When all the lifetime development and production costs are set forth in the life-cycle budget, management can set a price that will cover not only the company’s costs but also its required return on investment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 33.

2-10.q: A manufacturing firm has certain peak seasons, the summer season and the last two weeks of February. During these periods of increased output, the firm leases additional production equipment and hires additional temporary employees. Which one of the following budget techniques would best fit this firm’s needs?
o: Static budgeting because it shows the changes in sales or production levels
Project budgeting because discrete financial information is available
Zero-based budgeting because it is based on manager input
Flexible budgeting because it allows for adjustments to the budget based on actual activity levels
2-10.e: Flexible budgeting is the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. A flexible budget is prepared for the actual level of activity using all of the budgeted variable costs per unit along with the budgeted total fixed cost as determined at the beginning of the year. The flexible budget is the budget that would have been prepared for the actual level of activity for the period if the actual level of activity had been known in advance. Thus, flexible budgeting can adapt to changes in activity.
wrongWhy: Static budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
Project budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
Zero-based budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 34.

2-10.q: A municipal government requires each department supervisor to submit an annual budget request stating the specific goals of the department and listing a series of "decision packages" relating to each goal. Each decision package describes a set of desired activities, the benefits of these activities, and the potential consequences of not performing the activities. Funds are allocated based on the estimated costs and benefits of each package. This is an example of
2-10.e: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. Within zero-based budgeting all of the activities that a department undertakes are identified and then prioritized. This is the manner in which costs are justified and supported. Also, because the manager needs to examine every single expenditure and activity within the department, he is more likely to develop an alternative and, hopefully cheaper, method of accomplishing the same thing.
wrongWhy: An imposed budget is budget that is prepared by top management without the participation of lower level management.
Incremental budgeting is based on the prior period's budget.
A static budget is a budget that is prepared for only one level of activity. 35.

2-10.q: A company’s master budget projected the following information. If the company actually sold 27,500 units, the operating income when using a flexible budget would be
2-10.e: The flexible budget adjusts all variable revenues and expenses to the actual level of activity. That means adjusting revenues and the 2/3 portion of manufacturing costs that are variable ($80,000) while leaving the fixed costs (1/3 of the manufacturing costs, or $40,000, and all of the other operating costs) the same. The flexible budget would be: Revenue at 27,500 units $275,000 ($250,000 ÷ 25,000 × 27,500) Variable manufacturing costs 88,000 ($80,000 ÷ 25,000 × 27,500) Fixed manufacturing costs 40,000 (No change) Fixed other operating costs 100,000 (No change) Operating income $ 47,000
wrongWhy: This answer treats all costs as though they were variable costs. However, a portion (1/3) of the manufacturing costs are fixed and all of the other operating costs are fixed. The fixed costs do not change when the activity level changes as long as the activity level remains within the relevant range. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer results from treating the manufacturing costs as though 1/3 of them were variable and 2/3 were fixed. However, 1/3 of the manufacturing costs are fixed and 2/3 of them are variable. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This answer treats the manufacturing costs as though all of them were variable costs. However, a portion (1/3) of the manufacturing costs are fixed, and fixed costs do not change when the activity level changes as long as the activity level remains within the relevant range. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. 36.

2-10.q: RedRock Company uses flexible budgeting for cost control. RedRock produced 10,800 units of product during October, incurring indirect materials costs of $13,000. Its master budget for the year reflected indirect materials costs of $180,000 at a production volume of 144,000 units. A flexible budget for October production would reflect indirect materials costs of
2-10.e: Materials costs, even indirect materials costs, are variable costs. Therefore, the total cost of indirect materials fluctuates with the level of production. According to the master budget, the per unit cost of indirect material is $1.25 ($180,000 ÷ 144,000). Therefore, given an actual production of 10,800 units, the flexible budget costs for indirect material in October would be $13,500 ($1.25 × 10,800).
wrongWhy: This answer results from dividing the actual direct materials cost incurred during October ($13,000) by the budgeted monthly production level of $12,000 (144,000 ÷ 12), then multiplying the result by the 10,800 actual production level achieved. Since this calculation began with actual incurred cost, it cannot be the flexible budget amount.

This is not the correct answer. Please see the correct answer for a complete explanation. We have been unable to determine how to calculate this incorrect answer choice. If you have calculated it, please let us know how you did it so we can create a full explanation of why this answer choice is incorrect. actual incorrect answer choice -- not its letter, because that can change with every study session created. The Question ID number appears at the top of the question. Thank you in advance for helping us to make your HOCK study materials better.

This is the actual indirect materials cost. Indirect materials costs are variable costs. Therefore, the total cost of indirect materials fluctuates with the level of production. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. 37.

2-10.q: An organization that specializes in reviewing and editing technical magazine articles sets the following standards for evaluating the performance of the professional staff: Annual budgeted fixed costs for normal capacity level of 10,000 articles reviewed and edited: $600,000 Standard professional hours per 10 articles: 200 hours Flexible budget of standard labor costs to process 10,000 articles: $10,000,000 The following data apply to the 9,500 articles that were actually reviewed and edited during the current year: Total hours used by professional staff: 192,000 hours Variable costs: $9,120,000 Total cost: 9,738,000 Using a flexible budget, the total cost planned for the review and editing of 9,500 articles should be
2-10.e: The annual budgeted fixed costs for the normal capacity level of 10,000 articles reviewed and edited is $600,000 and it is the same for any other level of output, as fixed costs do not vary with the production level. The standard labor costs for the 9,500 articles is $9,500,000 [($10,000,000 ÷ 10,000) × 9,500]. The fixed costs plus the labor cost equal the total flexible budget cost planned for the review and editing of 9,500 articles: $600,000 + $9,500,000 = $10,100,000.
wrongWhy: Fixed costs are the same ($600,000) regardless to the level of production. This answer is calculated incorrectly by adjusting the fixed costs downward for production. Variable labor costs, however, change with the level of production and need to be $9,500,000 [($10,000,000 ÷ 10,000) × 9,500].
This is the variable labor costs only [($10,000,000 / 10,000) × 9,500].
Fixed costs are the same regardless to the level of production. This answer is calculated incorrectly by adjusting the fixed costs downward for production. 1.

2-11.q: The operating budget process usually begins with the
2-11.e: The sales budget is a part of operating budget and is usually the first budget to be prepared in the budgeting process. It is therefore the foundation of the operating budget.
wrongWhy: The budgeted balance sheet is a part of the financial budget, not the operating budget.
The financial budget is neither an operating budget nor a part of the operating budget.

The operating budget consists of the budgeted income statement and all the budgets that support it. Thus, essentially the budgeted income statement is the culmination of the process of developing the operating budget, not its beginning. 2.

2-11.q: The foundation of a profit plan is the
2-11.e: The sales forecast is usually the first forecast to be prepared in the budgeting process. After the sales level has been determined, the production budget and the other operating budgets can be prepared. Only after all of these other budgets are done can a budgeted income statement be prepared and profits estimated.
wrongWhy: The production plan cannot be calculated until the sales volume is forecast.
Cost and expense budgets cannot be calculated until the sales volume is forecast.

The capital budget is the budget in which all capital (property, plant and equipment) expenditures are planned. The capital expenditures budget is often prepared years in advance so that the company is able to obtain the necessary financing or accumulate the necessary cash to carry out its capital expansion plans. This budget is related to many of the current period operating budgets because they are affected by the capital projects planned for the budget year. However, it is not the foundation for the whole profit plan for the coming year. 3.

2-11.q: All of the following are part of the operating budget except for the
2-11.e: The capital expenditures budget is not a part of the operating budget. The capital budget is usually prepared separately from the other budgets. The capital budget concerns capital investments in plant, equipment, real estate, and so forth. Management plans these types of investments for years in advance and reviews them on an annual basis in order to allocate or obtain enough resources to perform them. Any capital expenditures to be made during the budget year will need to be included in the budgeting process for the year, however. Capital expenditures budgeted for the coming year will affect the budgeted balance sheet as increases in fixed assets and in accounts receivable, inventory, and accounts payable. They will affect the budgeted income statement as income expected from the new projects along with related expenses, including depreciation on the new equipment. Those effects on the income statement and the balance sheet will affect cash as well, so they will flow to the cash budget and the budgeted statement of cash flows.
wrongWhy: The direct materials usage budget is a part of the operating budget.
The sales budget is the first budget developed in the operating budget.
The production budget is a part of the operating budget. 4.

2-11.q: Many companies use comprehensive budgeting in planning for the next year's activities. When both an operating budget and a financial budget are prepared, which one of the following is correct concerning the financial budget? Included in the Financial Budget, respectively: Capital Budget, Master Budget Balance Sheet, Cash Budget
2-11.e: The financial budget includes the master budget balance sheet, the budgeted statement of cash flows, the cash budget, and the capital budget.
wrongWhy: The financial budget includes the master budget balance sheet, the budgeted statement of cash flows, the cash budget, and the capital budget. The capital budget is necessary as it demonstrates where large capital expenditures will be made and how capital will be used. If capital expenditures for the coming year will affect the balance sheet, income statement and cash flows, these capital expenditures must be reflected in the budgeted balance sheet, income statement and cash flow statement, so the capital budget is required in order to develop these other budgets.

The financial budget includes the master budget balance sheet, the budgeted statement of cash flows, the cash budget, and the capital budget. The capital budget shows where/how large amounts of capital will be utilized. The cash budget tracks the inflows and outflows of cash on a month-by- month (possibly even week-by-week or day-by-day) basis. The budgeted balance sheet shows how the entire budget is pulled together.

The financial budget includes the master budget balance sheet, the budgeted statement of cash flows, the cash budget, and the capital budget. The balance sheet is an important part of the financial budget as it pulls everything together at the end. 5.

2-11.q: Which one of the following may be considered an independent item in the preparation of the master budget?
2-11.e: The capital budget is the budget in which all capital (property, plant, and equipment) expenditures are planned. The capital expenditures budget is often prepared years in advance so that the company can plan to obtain the necessary financing or accumulate the necessary cash to carry out its capital expansion plans. The capital budget is an independent item in the development of the master budget. It is not a part of the annual budget development process. However, the capital budget is very important to the development of the annual master budget. The capital expenditures budget for the period covering the budget year must be in place before the master budget can be developed because the master budget needs to incorporate the capital expenditures planned for the budget year.
wrongWhy: The budgeted statement of financial position (balance sheet) is based on a number of elements of the master budget including the budgeted income statement, so it is not an independent item in the preparation of the master budget.
The desired level of ending inventory not independent, as it is used in the development of the production budget.

The budgeted income statement is a critical element of the master budget. It is one of the last budgets created from the operating budgets. The budgeted income statement is based on the sales budgets, expense budgets and other elements of the master budget, so it is not an independent item in the preparation of the master budget. 6.

2-11.q: The master budget
o: can be used to determine manufacturing cost variances
2-11.e: The master budget includes the operating budget, which consists of the budgeted income statement and all the budgets that support the budgeted income statement. The master budget also includes the financial budget, which consists of the capital expenditures budget, the cash budget, the budgeted balance sheet, and the budgeted statement of cash flows.
wrongWhy: The master budget is prepared for one level of activity and is developed before the budget period. The master budget consists of the financial budget and the operating budget. The operating budget includes all budgeted expenses whether or not they are controllable by specific, individual managers.

The master budget cannot be used to determine manufacturing cost variances. The master budget is prepared for one level of activity. Manufacturing variances are determined by comparing actual results with flexible budget amounts. A flexible budget is a budget that is prepared using the standard (budgeted) costs per unit produced and the actual level of production. The flexible budget is essentially what the budget would have been if the company had known what the actual level of production would be in advance and had used that information when it developed the budget.

The master budget is prepared for one level of activity and is developed before the budget period. The master budget consists of the financial budget and the operating budget. The master budget does not include actual results. 7.

2-11.q: A company operates on a calendar year but maintains four continuous quarterly budgets for up-to-date budget information. After completing its five-year long-term plan, the company’s management decided to make a capital investment in new equipment. The equipment is expected to have a useful life of nine years. Which one of the following is the most appropriate time period for the capital budget for the evaluation of the capital investment?
2-11.e: The capital budget covers multiple planning periods and an individual investment needs to be evaluated for a period covering the entire useful life of the investment.
wrongWhy: The capital budget covers multiple planning periods and an individual investment needs to be evaluated for a period covering the entire useful life of the investment.
The capital budget covers multiple planning periods and an individual investment needs to be evaluated for a period covering the entire useful life of the investment.
The capital budget covers multiple planning periods and an individual investment needs to be evaluated for a period covering the entire useful life of the investment. 1.

2-11.q: The First budget prepared in the master budget process is the:
2-11.e: Sales Budget is First — it is the foundation. All other operating budgets (production, DM, DL, OH) and eventually financial budgets depend on the sales forecast. Nothing can be planned until you know how much you expect to sell.

2-11.q: Production Budget formula: Units to Produce =
o: Budgeted Sales + Beginning Fg - Ending Fg
Budgeted Sales + Ending Fg - Beginning Fg
2-11.e: Units to Produce = Budgeted Sales + Desired Ending Fg Inventory - Beginning Fg Inventory. You need to produce enough to meet sales Plus build up desired ending inventory, minus what you already have at the start.

2-11.q: Budgeted Sales = 8,000 units. Desired Ending Fg = 1,500. Beginning Fg = 500. Units to Produce =
2-11.e: Units to Produce = 8,000 + 1,500 - 500 = 9,000 units.

2-11.q: The Direct Materials purchase budget differs from DM Usage because:
o: DM purchases adjusts for desired ending and beginning Rm inventory
DM usage includes only materials wasted
2-11.e: DM Usage = materials needed for production. DM Purchases = usage + Desired Ending Rm - Beginning Rm. You need to buy enough to cover production needs Plus build desired ending Rm inventory, minus what you already have.

2-11.q: The Direct Labor budget is calculated as:
o: Sales units × standard labor hours × standard rate
Production units × standard DL hours × standard rate per hour
2-11.e: DL Budget = Production Units × Standard DL Hours per unit × Standard Rate per hour. Uses Production units (not sales) because DL is incurred in the factory during production.

2-11.q: Selling & Administrative costs are classified as:
2-11.e: S&A costs are Period Costs — expensed when incurred, Not included in product cost or inventory. They appear on the income statement below gross profit. Manufacturing costs (DM, DL, Moh) are product costs.

2-11.q: The Budgeted Income Statement follows which format?
o: Sales Revenue - COGS = Gross Profit - S&A = Operating Income
2-11.e: Budgeted Income Statement: Sales Revenue - Budgeted COGS (DM+DL+Moh) = Gross Profit - S&A Expenses = Operating Income. Follows the same format as the actual income statement but uses Budgeted (planned) numbers.

2-11.q: Moh Budget: Variable OH rate $3/Dlh, budgeted Dlh = 5,000, fixed OH = $20,000. Budgeted Moh =
2-11.e: Budgeted Moh = (Variable Rate × Activity) + Fixed OH = ($3 × 5,000) + $20,000 = $15,000 + $20,000 = $35,000.

2-11.q: Production = 10,000 units. Standard DM = 3 lbs/unit at $4/lb. Desired Ending Rm = 5,000 lbs. Beginning Rm = 8,000 lbs. DM Purchases (lbs) =
2-11.e: DM needed for production = 10,000 × 3 = 30,000 lbs. DM to purchase = 30,000 + 5,000 (desired ending) - 8,000 (beginning) = 27,000 lbs.

2-11.q: DM to purchase = 27,000 lbs at $4/lb. Budgeted DM cost =
2-11.e: Budgeted DM cost = 27,000 lbs × $4/lb = $108,000.

2-11.q: After preparing the production budget, the Next step is the:
2-11.e: Budget sequence: Sales → Production → Direct Materials (and DL, Moh budgets prepared simultaneously) → S&A → Budgeted Income Statement → Cash Budget → Capital Budget → Budgeted Balance Sheet. DM budget follows production because you need production units to determine material needs.

2-11.q: DM Usage (for production) = Production units × Standard DM per unit. With 9,000 units and standard of 3 lbs/unit:
2-11.e: DM Usage = 9,000 units × 3 lbs/unit = 27,000 lbs. This is the material Needed For Production. DM Purchases may differ because of inventory adjustments (+ Ending Rm desired - Beginning Rm).

2-11.q: DM Purchases = DM Usage + Desired Ending Rm - Beginning Rm. Usage = 27,000 lbs, Ending Rm desired = 4,000 lbs, Beginning Rm = 2,000 lbs. DM Purchases =

2-12.q: Superflite expects April sales of its deluxe model airplane, the C-14, to be 402,000 units at $11 each. Each C-14 requires three purchased components shown below. Purchase Number Needed Cost for each C-14 Unit Factory direct labor and variable overhead per unit of C-14 totals $3.00. Fixed factory overhead is $1.00 per unit at a production level of 500,000 units. Superflite plans the following beginning and ending inventories for the month of April and uses standard absorption costing for valuing inventory. Units at Units at Part No. April 1 April 30 The C-14 production budget for April should be based on the manufacture of
2-12.e: To solve these question we should use the formula of physical flow of finished goods inventory: Beginning Inventory + Units Produced − Units Sold = Ending Inventory. Whenever we have three of these amounts, we can always find the fourth. In this problem, Plugging numbers for C-14 into the formula, we get the following: 12,000 + Units Produced − 402,000 = 10,000 Solving for Units Produced, we get Units Produced = 400,000 units.
wrongWhy: This is the number of unit sales. It does not take the change in inventory level into consideration. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

This is the beginning inventory plus the ending inventory plus the number of units expected to be sold. To solve these question we should use the formula of physical flow of finished goods inventory: Beginning Inventory + Units Produced − Units Sold = Ending Inventory. Whenever we have three of these amounts, we can always find the fourth. In this problem, Units Produced is the amount needed.
This answer ignores the requirements to have some ending inventory on hand. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. 2.

2-12.q: Superflite expects April sales of its deluxe model airplane, the C-14, to be 402,000 units at $11 each. Each C-14 requires three purchased components shown below. Purchase Number Needed Cost for each C-14 Unit Factory direct labor and variable overhead per unit of C-14 totals $3.00. Fixed factory overhead is $1.00 per unit at a production level of 500,000 units. Superflite plans the following beginning and ending inventories for the month of April and uses standard absorption costing for valuing inventory. Units at Units at Part No. April 1 April 30 Assume Superflite plans to manufacture 400,000 units in April. The total April budget for all purchased components should be
2-12.e: To solve these question we should use the formula for the physical flow of raw materials inventory to calculate the amount to be purchased for each component (A-9, B-6 and D- 28), then use the price of each component to calculate the total amount to be budgeted for April's production. Beginning Inventory + Units Purchased − Units Used in Production = Ending Inventory For A-9: one unit of A-9 is necessary for each unit of finished product. Plugging numbers for A-9 into the formula we will get the following: 21,000 + Units Purchased − (400,000 × 1) = 9,000. Solving for Units Purchased, we get Units Purchased = 388,000. The cost to purchase 388,000 units of A-9 is $194,000 ($0.50 × 388,000). For B-6: two units of B-6 are necessary for each unit of finished product. Plugging numbers for B-6 into the formula we will get the following: 32,000 + Units Purchased − (400,000 × 2) = 10,000. Solving for Units Purchased, we get Units Purchased = 778,000. The cost to purchase 778,000 units of B-6 is $194,500 ($0.25 × 778,000). For D-28: three units of D-28 are necessary for each unit of finished product. Plugging numbers for D-28 into the formula we get the following: 14,000 + Units Purchased − (400,000 × 3) = 6,000. Solving for Units Purchased, we get Units Purchased = 1,192,000. The cost to purchase 1,192,000 units of D-28 is $1,192,000 ($1.00 × 1,192,000). Adding the costs to purchase all of the components together, we get $1,580,500 ($194,000 + $194,500 + $1,192,000).
wrongWhy: This answer results from a mathematical error made in calculating the units of D-28 to be purchased. The correct formula is Beginning Inventory + Units Purchased − Units Used in Production = Ending Inventory. However, the formula used to calculate the units purchased of D-28 that results in this answer is Beginning Inventory − Units Purchased + Units Used in Production = Ending Inventory.

This answer is calculated using the units to be produced (400,000) only. It does not take into consideration the beginning and ending inventories of the three raw materials to be used. 3.

2-12.q: What would be the correct chronological order of preparation for the following budgets? I. Cost of goods sold budget. II. Production budget. III. Purchases budget. IV. Administrative (nonmanufacturing) budget.
2-12.e: The production budget indicates what needs to be produced and when, so this is information required for the purchases budget. The COGS budget is based on desired beginning inventory, desired ending inventory, and expected purchases, so the purchases budget must be prepared before the COGS budget can be prepared. The administrative budget is last as some variable administrative expenses such as purchasing expenses will be based on the previous budgets.
wrongWhy: The administrative budget must come last in this series, as some variable administrative expenses such as purchasing expenses will be based on the previous budgets.
The purchases budget cannot be completed before the production budget is complete.
The COGS budget cannot be completed before the production budget and purchases budgets are complete. 4.

2-12.q: The production budget process usually begins with the
2-12.e: The production budget is the number of units to be produced. It is impossible to prepare a production or master budget until the sales budget has been prepared because without the sales budget, the company cannot know how many units it will need to produce. Thus the production budget process begins with the sales budget.
wrongWhy: The direct materials budget cannot be prepared until it is determined how much of product is going to be produced, and that is the production budget. The production budget is the number of units to be produced. Thus the production budget must be prepared before the direct materials budget can be developed.

The manufacturing overhead costs budget cannot be prepared until it is determined how much of product is going to be produced, and that is the production budget. The production budget is the number of units to be produced. Thus the production budget must be prepared before the manufacturing overhead budget can be developed.

The direct labor budget cannot be prepared until it is determined how much of product is going to be produced, and that is the production budget. The production budget is the number of units to be produced. Thus the production budget must be prepared before the direct labor budget can be developed. 5.

2-12.q: Which one of the following statements regarding selling and administrative budgets is most accurate?
o: Selling and administrative budgets are usually optional
Selling and administrative budgets need to be detailed so that the key assumptions can be better understood
Selling and administrative budgets are fixed in nature
Selling and administrative budgets are difficult to allocate by month and are best presented as one number for the entire year
2-12.e: Selling and administrative budgets should be detailed enough to be useful, including an explanation of the underlying assumptions. These assumptions should be documented so that if they are changed it will be easier to adjust the affected budget figures.
wrongWhy: Selling and administrative budgets are not usually optional.
Selling and administrative budgets are not fixed in nature.
Selling and administrative budgets are not difficult to allocate by month and are not necessarily best presented as one number for the entire year. 6.

2-12.q: When preparing the annual operating budget, management usually starts the process with the
2-12.e: The sales budget is the first operating budget to be prepared.
wrongWhy: The capital budget is not a part of the operating budget. It is a part of the financial budget. The capital budget is a long-term budget and is prepared outside of the annual budgeting process. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

The cash budget is not a part of the operating budget. It is a part of the financial budget. The cash budget cannot be prepared until all of the operating budgets have been developed. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

The balance sheet is not a part of the operating budget. It is a part of the financial budget. It can be prepared only after all of the individual operating budgets have been prepared. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. 7.

2-12.q: Rock Industries has four divisions. In the quest to develop a more achievable budget for the coming year, the chief executive officer has elected to develop the company's budget by using a decentralized bottom-up budget approach. Chip Jones is production manager in one of the divisions. Jones’ involvement in the budget process this year will probably
o: Require development of a production budget after receiving the division's projected sales forecast
Require development of a production budget based on the prior year's manufacturing activity
Require development of a production budget that is forwarded to the Budget Department
2-12.e: Jones will develop a budget for his production department after determining how much is expected to be sold. The production budget will take into account what is to be sold as well as cushions for extra sales and provisions for ending finished goods.
wrongWhy: While prior year activity is one place to start, it does not include the sales forecast for the upcoming year or adjustments for changes in costs or processes.

This is incorrect. Jones will develop a production budget that is forwarded to the Budget Department; however, first he will have to have an idea of how much to produce. This will come from the sales forecast.
In a bottom up budget, the department managers will have a significant role in budget development. 8.

Accounts payable at end of 1st quarter
Accounts payable at end of 2nd quarter
All 2nd quarter costs and expenses (accrual basis)
